      "risk": "If step 2 (updateStatus) or step 3 (sendDispatchAlert) throws, the courier is reserved but the order is never marked ASSIGNED. No other order can use that courier, and no retry of this function will release them because reserve() will allocate a NEW courier.",
      "severity": "Critical",
      "location": "assignAndDispatch — between steps 1 and 2, and between steps 2 and 3",
      "reproduction": "1. Retry assignAndDispatch('order-1') — reserves courier-B, compounding the leak.",
      "fix": "Wrap in try/catch with compensating actions: on failure after step 1, call courierService.release(courierId). On failure after step 2, also revert orderService.updateStatus back to previous state. Alternatively, implement a saga/orchestrator pattern with explicit compensation steps for each mutation.",
      "time_to_fix": "1-2 hours",
      "acceptance_criteria_affected": "Courier availability, order assignment reliability, system consistency"
    },
    {
      "title": "No idempotency — retries double-allocate couriers",
      "risk": "If a caller retries assignAndDispatch for the same orderId (e.g., after a timeout where the first call actually succeeded), reserve() allocates a second courier. Now two couriers are dispatched to the same order, or one is orphaned. There is no check whether the order already has a courier assigned.",
      "severity": "Critical",
      "location": "assignAndDispatch — step 1 (courierService.reserve)",
      "reproduction": "1. Call assignAndDispatch('order-1') — succeeds fully, courier-A assigned. 2. Caller's HTTP layer times out and retries. 3. Second call reserves courier-B for order-1. 4. Two couriers dispatched, or courier-B orphaned.",
      "fix": "Add an idempotency guard at the top: check if the order already has an assigned courier before reserving. courierService.reserve should itself be idempotent — if orderId already has a reservation, return the existing courierId instead of allocating a new one.",
      "time_to_fix": "30 minutes - 1 hour",
      "acceptance_criteria_affected": "Courier utilization, order correctness, cost (paying two couriers)"
    },
    {
      "title": "Notification failure silently blocks dispatch completion with no fallback",
      "risk": "If sendDispatchAlert (step 3) fails, the courier is reserved and the order is marked ASSIGNED, but the courier never receives the dispatch notification — they don't know they have a delivery. The customer notification (step 4) also never fires. The order appears assigned in the system but no one is actually coming. There is no retry queue, no dead-letter mechanism, and no monitoring to detect this silent failure state.",
      "severity": "High",
      "location": "assignAndDispatch — steps 3 and 4",
      "reproduction": "1. Steps 1-2 succeed. 2. Notification service is temporarily down. 3. sendDispatchAlert throws. 4. Order status is ASSIGNED, courier is reserved, but neither party is notified. 5. Customer waits indefinitely.",
      "fix": "Decouple notifications from the critical assignment path. Steps 3 and 4 should be fire-and-forget with a retry queue (or at minimum wrapped in individual try/catch blocks that log failures and enqueue retries). The assignment itself (steps 1-2) should not roll back due to notification failure.",
      "time_to_fix": "1-2 hours",
      "acceptance_criteria_affected": "Courier dispatch reliability, customer experience, SLA compliance"
    }
  ],

  "important_issues": [
    {
      "title": "No timeout boundaries on any service call",
      "description": "Each await could hang indefinitely. If courierService.reserve hangs for 30 seconds, the entire flow is blocked with no circuit breaker or timeout. Under load, this queues up callers and cascades into resource exhaustion.",
      "location": "assignAndDispatch — all 4 await calls"
    },
    {
      "title": "Return type void hides outcome from caller",
      "description": "The function returns Promise<void>, giving the caller no way to know which courier was assigned or what state was reached. On partial failure, the caller cannot perform targeted recovery because they have no handle on the courierId that was reserved.",
      "location": "assignAndDispatch — return type"
    }
  ],

  "suggestions": [
    "Return a result object { courierId, orderId, status } so callers and retry logic can act on partial completion state",
    "Add structured logging at each step boundary to enable post-incident reconstruction of partial failures",
    "Consider making steps 3-4 eventually consistent via an event/queue rather than synchronous in the critical path"
  ],

  "production_readiness": {
    "deployment_risks": "Any deployment of this code will produce orphaned courier reservations under normal failure rates. Even 0.1% failure rate on step 2 means leaked couriers proportional to order volume.",

    "backward_compatibility": "No backward compatibility concerns — this appears to be a new function.",
    "monitoring_coverage": "No logging, no metrics, no alerting. Orphaned couriers and failed notifications will be invisible until customers complain.",

  },

content.thinking:
Let me analyze this code and produce my review output.

Classification: `none` — the fixes are standard error handling patterns (compensation/rollback logic, try-catch, idempotency). No architectural concerns.

Key hypotheses:
1. No compensation/rollback on partial failure — if step 2 fails, courier is reserved but order not updated (orphaned reservation)
2. No idempotency — double-call reserves two couriers for same order
3. No timeout/retry — any step hanging blocks everything with no recovery

Let me write the output directly.
